summary: I was born in Rockford, Illinois, at Saint Anthony Hospital on April 25th, 1920. My early life was deeply connected to Rockford, where my father worked and where I graduated from Rockford High School in 1937. Later, I enlisted at Camp Grant in Rockford during World War II, marking a significant chapter in my life.

Saint Anthony Hospital in Rockford is where I was born and later where I began my nurse's training in August 1938. I loved my time there, especially working in the laboratory and starting the blood bank with Dr. Matthews. This hospital was the foundation of my nursing career and my first real connection to the medical field.

My father, John Elof Anderson, was an immigrant from Sweden who came to America through Ellis Island. He was advised to go by his middle name because there were so many John Andersons from Sweden. His Swedish roots were an important part of our family story.

Ellis Island was the entry point for my father when he immigrated from Sweden. It was there that officials suggested he use his middle name to distinguish himself from others. This moment was a key part of our family's journey to America.

My father was a furniture-maker who worked for the Union Furniture Company. He also did a lot of furniture work in our home after he and my mother married. His craftsmanship was a steady part of our family life in Rockford.

The Great Depression affected my family deeply; when I was 14, my father had just gotten a job again after the hard times. It was a difficult period, but my aunt helped me through it after my father passed away. The economic struggles shaped much of my early life.

After graduating high school in 1937, I worked in the office at Burson Knitting Company. I earned 25 cents an hour, but during the slack season and the following spring, I was laid off along with everyone else in the office. This job was a stepping stone before I entered nurse's training.

Michigan became important to me during World War II when I was assigned to Percy Jones Hospital in Battle Creek. I also made lifelong friends with two roommates from Michigan during my nurse's training. My time in Michigan was marked by intense nursing work and preparation for military service.

World War II was a defining period in my life. I trained as a nurse and eventually enlisted in the Army, serving at Percy Jones Hospital and later with the 86th Field Hospital. The war influenced everything from my work with wounded soldiers to my deployment in the Pacific theater.

During my nurse training, the sisters at Saint Anthony Hospital were originally from Germany and sent care packages to relatives there. We heard many stories about the situation in Germany, though we didn't fully understand it at the time. The war made Germany a distant but significant presence in our lives.

I enlisted in the Army with two roommates at Camp Grant in Rockford during World War II. Despite initial hurdles like being underweight, I signed a release and was accepted. The Army assigned me to Percy Jones Hospital and later to the 86th Field Hospital, shaping my military nursing career.

Camp Grant in Rockford was where I enlisted in the Army with my roommates. We underwent examinations and paperwork there before waiting for orders. It was the starting point of my military service during World War II.

I was assigned to Percy Jones Hospital in Battle Creek, Michigan, in July 1943. It had been the Kellogg Sanitarium and was a beautiful facility where I worked on orthopedic and neuromuscular wards. I also participated in penicillin research there, which was groundbreaking at the time.

Battle Creek was where Percy Jones Hospital was located, where I served as a nurse during World War II. The hospital was formerly the Kellogg Sanitarium, and I spent a year and a half there caring for wounded soldiers. It was a pivotal place in my nursing and military career.

Percy Jones Hospital, where I worked, was formerly the Kellogg Sanitarium in Battle Creek, Michigan. The building was beautiful with excellent facilities, which impressed me when I arrived in 1943. This history added a unique character to the hospital environment.
cues: Former name of Percy Jones Hospital

summary: Chicago was where the 86th Field Hospital was formed, and where my roommate and I met with others before deployment. We flew from Chicago to Seattle as part of our journey to the Pacific theater. Chicago was a key transit point in my military service.

Seattle was our arrival point after leaving Chicago, where we received warm clothing before learning we were headed to the Pacific, not Europe. We stayed at Fort Lawton for basic training and medical shots, and waited there for our orders to embark. Seattle marked the beginning of our overseas deployment.

At Fort Lawton near Seattle, we finally received basic training, which we hadn't had before enlisting. We learned to climb rope ladders, crawl through obstacle courses, and marched extensively. It was also where we met the officers of the 86th Field Hospital before heading overseas.

We were based on Oahu at Koko Head, a medical training place in Hawaii, after arriving in the Pacific. There we lived in comfortable tents, did marching drills, hikes, and training courses like target practice and plane identification. We also toured the island and visited Pearl Harbor and Hickam Field during our stay.

While stationed on Oahu, we visited Pearl Harbor, which at the time was not as famous as it is now. It was part of our tours of the island during our medical training stay in Hawaii. Seeing Pearl Harbor gave us a sense of the war's realities in the Pacific.

During our time on Oahu, we also visited Hickam Field, one of the major military installations on the island. It was part of the broader experience of being stationed in Hawaii during the war. These visits helped us understand the scale of the military presence there.

Easter Sunday, April 1st, 1945, was a memorable day because while we were in chapel on Oahu, we were interrupted with the news that Okinawa had been invaded. This marked a turning point as the men prepared to leave immediately for the battle. It was a solemn and significant moment during my service.

The Battle of Okinawa began on Easter Sunday, April 1st, 1945, while we were stationed in Hawaii. Shortly after the invasion, we received orders to board ship and head to Okinawa as part of the 86th Field Hospital. The battle was a major event that shaped the final stages of the war in the Pacific.

On our way to Okinawa aboard the Florence Nightingale, we stopped at Ulithi Atoll where we were allowed to swim. The island had been shelled and was devastated, with no standing palm trees, but it featured beautiful sand beaches. It was a brief respite during our journey through the Pacific.

Kwajalein Atoll was another stop on our voyage to Okinawa where we could get out and swim. Like Ulithi, it had been shelled and was devastated but had beautiful sand beaches. These stops were rare moments of relief during our tense journey.

While we were on the ship heading to Okinawa, it was announced over the loudspeaker that President Franklin D. Roosevelt had died. This news caused much speculation about the war's future, as we had never heard of his successor, Harry S. Truman. Roosevelt's death was a significant moment during our deployment.
cues: Death announced en route to Okinawa

summary: After President Roosevelt's death, Harry S. Truman became our new president while we were en route to Okinawa. We had not heard of him before, and there were many rumors and uncertainties about how he would handle the war. His presidency marked a new chapter during our service.

The Florence Nightingale was the ship that carried us from Seattle to Okinawa. It was full of nurses and corpsmen, and we endured rough seas and heat during the voyage. We drilled regularly for emergencies, but thankfully never had to use those skills.

I was assigned to the 86th Field Hospital, which formed in Chicago before deploying to the Pacific. My roommate and I joined this unit, traveling through Seattle, Hawaii, and eventually to Okinawa. The 86th was our home during the latter part of the war.

The 76th Field Hospital was mentioned as one of the groups of nurses aboard the Florence Nightingale along with our 86th Field Hospital unit. We traveled together on the ship heading to Okinawa. It was part of the broader medical support network in the Pacific.

The United States Navy provided gun ships to escort our transport ship through submarine-infested waters in the Pacific. Their protection was crucial during our voyage to Okinawa, especially given the threat of Japanese submarines near Hawaii. The Navy's role ensured our safe passage.
